constructive_proposals: Question witches on Holy Days and during Mass
Exhort people to pray for Divine help and invoke Saints' prayers
Judge wears Consecrated Salt, Seven Words of Christ, Relics of the Saints
Bind Holy things around judge's body
Give accused Holy Water to drink before questioning
Read depositions of witnesses aloud during interrogation
Bring witnesses face-to-face with accused to shame her into confession
Offer ordeal by red-hot iron as test of innocence (though explicitly forbidden)
Treat accused well in food and drink, engage honest persons in conversation
Promise mercy conditionally, with mental reservation for State safety
Record all promises made by Judge in writing
Examine friends and associates without accused's knowledge
Show instruments, unguents, boxes to accused and ask their purpose
Have trusted man engage accused in prolonged conversation at night
Use spies to listen to conversations between accused and suspected accomplice
Hear confession immediately without delay, even at night
Do not postpone confession due to interruptions or delays

scapegoat.name: Witches
scapegoat.justification: The text portrays witches as agents of the Devil who deceive others, resist confession despite overwhelming evidence, and threaten both religious and civil order. Their very existence is framed as a plague requiring ritualistic, coercive, and punitive measures to expose and eradicate.

moral_justification: The moral justification presented is that protecting the State and upholding divine order requires the eradication of witchcraft through any means necessary — including psychological pressure, religious ritual, torture, and false promises — because witches are inherently dangerous, unrepentant, and aligned with evil forces.
